vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on shadow File
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/shadow, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0000 /etc/shadow

triage_decision.reason: Correcting permissions on /etc/shadow to restrict access is a standard STIG hardening procedure. The recommended command removes all permissions for all users, which is the standard way to ensure only the root user (via specific processes) can access sensitive password hashes. This does not break system functionality as long as the file remains owned by root.
triage_decision.estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrectly applying permissions could theoretically impact specific legacy services that read shadow directly, though this is rare on modern RHEL 9 systems.

review_feedback: Setting /etc/shadow permissions to 0000 makes the file unreadable even for the root user. While root can technically change permissions back, many critical system processes (PAM, sshd, shadow-utils) require read access to /etc/shadow to authenticate users. Even if run as root, setting it to 0000 can cause immediate authentication failures for all users and break system services that rely on password verification.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: The plan will resolve the vulnerability by removing all access to the file. However, setting permissions to 0000 is extremely restrictive. While it satisfies some hardening standards, it can sometimes interfere with specific legacy tools or monitoring agents that expect at least read access for root. A more standard approach is 0600 or 0400 (root only).
concerns: Setting permissions to 0000 makes the file completely unreadable even by the root user without a chmod change, which might complicate automated processes or debugging.
The plan does not explicitly check ownership of the file, which is critical for the security of /etc/shadow.
suggested_improvements: Verify that the file is owned by root:user before changing permissions.
Consider using 'chmod 600 /etc/shadow' or 'chmod 400 /etc/shadow' instead of 0000 to allow the root user to read the file without needing to change permissions back first.
Add a check to ensure the file exists before attempting to change permissions.
